text: Optical neural networks (ONNs) that implement abstract photonic computing have been shown to excel in real-time image processing with much higher speeds and energy efficiencies 1–5 . However, much of the existing research on ONNs is focused on developing and optimizing new, often highly complex architectures of optical circuits and therefore the experimental implementation of real ONN-based systems for scalable and practical applications is unclear due to non-trivial optical nonlinearities, analog-to-digital converter (ADC) power consumption following optical signal processing, and sensitivity to noise and artifacts 1,6–8 . Here, an all-analog photonic chip is proposed and realized as an all-analog optoelectronic Chip Combining electronic and light computing (ACCEL). ACCEL exhibits an integrated energy efficiency of 74.8 peta-operations per second per watt, with a processing throughput of 4.6 peta-operations per second that is comprised of over 99% purely optical operations. In particular, we utilized diffractive optical computing to operate the optically encoded diffraction patterns as an analogue optical feature extractor. The photocurrents generated from light at the photodiodes are then utilized to perform subsequent analogue computing operations in an integrated analogue computing circuit on the same photonic chip, thereby eradicating the need for ADCs and reducing computing latency per frame to just 72 ns. Achieving optoelectronic computing synergy and training adaptation lead to accurate performances reaching 85.5% (Fashion-MNIST), 82% (3-class ImageNet) and 92.6% (time-lapse video classification) and significantly enhance system robustness especially under low-light conditions that achieve as low as 0.14 fJ μm −2 energy density per frame. It is envisioned that ACCEL can be widely used for numerous applications, including that of wearable devices, autonomous vehicles and industrial remote sensing.
